summary: Joe Reis explores the hype around AI agents in 2025, arguing that while they show impressive demos, they face a fundamental obstacle: the poor state of corporate data. Text-to-SQL remains immature and hallucinates on complex queries, and most enterprise datasets are too messy to expose to LLMs without significant cleanup.
key_points: AI agents like Claude Computer Use show impressive autonomous task completion capabilities
Text-to-SQL technology is still immature and struggles with complex queries
Corporate data quality issues are forcing companies to address past data sins
The 'year of AI agents' narrative may be driven by Big Tech's need to recoup massive AI investments
AI is driving renewed interest in data quality, governance, and data modeling
money_quotes: And as you know, most corporate datasets are utter hellscapes, ranging from poorly named columns to janky data models to lord knows what the fuck is happening.
Very few people raise their hands when I ask audiences if they'd throw an LLM atop their existing data.
If anything, attention to AI forces companies to reckon with their past data sins.
I'm not bullish on short-term bullshit, but I'm long-term bullish on AI.
Perhaps Big Tech is trying to repay its massive investments in model training and expansion of data centers. Or maybe they need another narrative to keep the stock price high?
key_insight: AI agents face a fundamental bottleneck in enterprise adoption: the chaotic state of corporate data means companies must first fix their data foundations before AI can deliver on its promises.
